title: Real estate enumerations
description: Enumerations specific to the real estate domain

PropertyType.description: Classification of the real estate property. OFFICE: commercial space leased to businesses for professional and administrative use. RETAIL: properties leased to tenants for consumer-facing sales and services, such as shopping centers and storefronts. INDUSTRIAL: warehouses, distribution centers, and manufacturing facilities used for production, storage, or logistics. MULTIFAMILY: residential buildings with multiple dwelling units leased to individual tenants. HOSPITALITY: hotels, resorts, and other lodging properties generating revenue primarily from nightly room rates. MIXED_USE: properties combining two or more uses (e.g., retail, office, residential) within a single development. DATA_CENTER: facilities designed to house computing infrastructure, servers, and networking equipment with specialized power and cooling. LIFE_SCIENCE: laboratory and research space purpose-built for biotech, pharmaceutical, or medical device companies. SELF_STORAGE: facilities offering individual storage units rented to consumers and businesses on flexible terms. STUDENT_HOUSING: purpose-built residential properties catering to college and university students, often leased by the bed. SENIOR_LIVING: residential communities providing housing and varying levels of care for elderly residents. HEALTHCARE: medical offices, outpatient clinics, hospitals, and other facilities used for the delivery of healthcare services.

PropertyStatus.description: Current lifecycle status of the property. ACQUISITION: property has been identified or is under contract for purchase but not yet operationally managed. DEVELOPMENT: property is under construction or undergoing major ground-up development. STABILIZED: property is substantially leased and generating steady-state income at or near market occupancy. VALUE_ADD: property is undergoing improvements, lease-up, or repositioning to increase cash flow and value. REPOSITIONING: property is being fundamentally transformed in use, tenant mix, or market positioning to capture higher returns. DISPOSITION: property is actively being marketed for sale or is under contract to be sold. SOLD: property has been divested and the transaction has closed.

LeaseType.description: Classification of the lease structure determining how operating expenses are allocated between landlord and tenant. GROSS: landlord pays all operating expenses (taxes, insurance, maintenance); tenant pays a single gross rent. NET: tenant pays base rent plus one category of operating expense, typically property taxes. DOUBLE_NET: tenant pays base rent plus property taxes and building insurance; landlord retains responsibility for structural maintenance. TRIPLE_NET: tenant pays base rent plus all three major operating expenses — property taxes, insurance, and maintenance — giving the landlord a net-of-expenses income stream. MODIFIED_GROSS: a hybrid structure where landlord and tenant negotiate a custom split of operating expenses, often with the tenant covering increases above a base-year amount. PERCENTAGE: tenant pays a base rent plus a percentage of gross sales revenue above a specified breakpoint, commonly used in retail leases.

ValuationMethod.description: Methodology used for a property valuation. APPRAISAL: value determined by a certified appraiser's professional opinion. COMPARABLE_SALES: value derived from recent sales of similar properties in the market. DCF: discounted cash flow analysis projecting future income streams to present value. REPLACEMENT_COST: value based on the cost to reconstruct the property with equivalent utility.

LeaseOptionType.description: Type of option embedded within a lease agreement. RENEWAL: tenant's right to extend the lease for an additional term. EXPANSION: tenant's right to lease additional space within the property. TERMINATION: tenant's right to end the lease before its scheduled expiration. RIGHT_OF_FIRST_REFUSAL: tenant's right to match any third-party offer on the space or property. PURCHASE: tenant's right to buy the leased property at predetermined or market terms.

VehicleType.description: Type of real estate investment vehicle structure. OPEN_END_FUND: a perpetual-life fund that accepts new subscriptions and redemptions on a periodic basis. CLOSED_END_FUND: a fixed-life fund with a defined fundraising period and no ongoing subscriptions. REIT: a real estate investment trust, typically publicly traded, that owns and operates income-producing properties. JOINT_VENTURE: a co-investment structure between two or more parties for a specific property or portfolio. SEPARATE_ACCOUNT: a dedicated investment account managed on behalf of a single investor. CLUB_DEAL: a small group of investors pooling capital for a specific investment opportunity.

VehicleStrategy.description: Investment strategy of the real estate vehicle. CORE: low-risk, stabilized assets with predictable income and minimal leverage. CORE_PLUS: primarily stabilized assets with modest value enhancement opportunities and moderate leverage. VALUE_ADD: assets requiring operational improvements, repositioning, or lease-up to increase value. OPPORTUNISTIC: high-risk, high-return strategies including development, distressed assets, or complex transactions. DEVELOPMENT: ground-up construction or major redevelopment of real estate assets.

VehicleStatus.description: Current lifecycle status of the investment vehicle. FUNDRAISING: actively raising capital from investors. INVESTING: deploying committed capital into property acquisitions. STABILIZED: portfolio is fully invested and generating steady returns. HARVESTING: disposing of assets and returning capital to investors. LIQUIDATED: all assets sold and capital returned; vehicle is wound down.

AppraisalApproach.description: Appraisal methodology employed for a formal property appraisal. INCOME: value derived from capitalizing the property's net operating income stream. SALES_COMPARISON: value determined by analyzing recent sales of comparable properties. COST: value based on land value plus the depreciated cost of improvements. COMBINED: a blended approach using two or more of the above methodologies to reconcile a final value.
